title: Offices of Physical, Occupational and Speech Therapists, and Audiologists

narrative.executive_view: This code is a credible outpatient-rehabilitation consolidation market but a difficult AI-retention thesis. The clinic product is licensed, hands-on therapist time; AI documentation and revenue-cycle tools can free non-billable time, but replacing clinical minutes can remove the billable unit rather than create a wage saving. CMS's 85% payment rule for qualifying PTA/OTA services is a concrete reminder that payer rules, not AI, already shape economics [S1]. The practical case is a conditional, management-and-density roll-up with modest AI-enabled throughput rather than a clinical labor-replacement play.
narrative.how_ai_changes_work: The clean AI surface is scheduling, eligibility verification, prior-authorisation paperwork, claims/denials, intake and documentation support. AI scribes can reclaim therapist note-writing time and potentially add visits if capacity and demand exist; they do not make manual therapy, clinical judgment or licensed accountability disappear. PT, OT, speech and assistant roles are therefore assigned low task-replaceability fractions, while front office and selected management are high. WebPT's 2025 report describes increasing AI interest aimed at reducing operational friction and administrative burden, not clinical replacement [S2].
narrative.value_capture: AI value is hard to retain because payers buy services through fee schedules, contracts, authorisation rules and visit limits. CMS requires reduced payment for qualifying PTA/OTA-furnished services [S1], illustrating a structural payer capture channel independent of AI. A clinic can retain some documentation-driven throughput if it has referral demand, therapist capacity and payer authorization, but the public evidence does not prove a stable price uplift. I therefore use modest capture inputs and put payer capture in C only; the human clinical floor supports terminal demand, not price retention.
narrative.adoption_timing: Effective adoption should become common within about four years, led by documentation, revenue-cycle and workflow products. WebPT's survey release says AI adoption is increasing and respondents see technology as a way to reduce administrative burden [S2]. Current effective adoption is estimated at 20%, not inferred from vendor marketing. The timing estimate is deliberately separate from the attractive consolidation evidence and from therapist-labor scarcity.
narrative.consolidation_economics: There is clear scale activity: Provident's PT review says fragmentation, supply-demand imbalance and consolidator competition are catalyzing investment and reports historical private PT transactions at 10.4x-16.1x EBITDA for $12M-$175M EBITDA targets [S4]. Those are platform-scale historical comparables, not valid purchase quotes for a $1-10M EBITDA clinic. The 4.5x entry is deliberately well below those historical platform indications, while the 10.0x exit assumes verified multi-site scale and payer diversification. Both are bounded estimates, not observed band-size prints; actual underwriting needs local payer mix, normalized owner-therapist compensation, referral concentration and clinic-level cohort data.
narrative.terminal_demand: Terminal demand is DURABLE but pressured. Licensed hands-on rehabilitation, referral relationships and in-person clinical judgment remain paid needs; AI cannot itself deliver manual treatment. Conversely, digital MSK programs compete for some low-acuity utilization: Hinge's company-sponsored, validated claims analysis reported lower physical or occupational therapy utilization alongside other cost reductions [S5]. That supports a bounded substitution risk, not a claim that all outpatient therapy melts. It is kept distinct from capture and from V.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The code is heterogeneous: orthopaedic PT, pediatric speech, OT, audiology with hearing-aid economics, and mixed clinic groups have different reimbursement and labor economics. The supplied band includes both 100-499 and 500+ employer classes, so it is not a direct map to a single local clinic. APTA reported a 9.5% national vacancy rate in outpatient PT practices in 2024 and roughly 13% of PT/PTA positions open [S3]; this supports labor scarcity but not owner-age succession. The main open questions are payer mix, local referral durability, documentation-to-visit conversion, and whether an exit buyer will credit scale without discounting reimbursement exposure.

sources.title: CMS Therapy Services
sources.what_it_supports: Medicare therapy payment mechanics, including the 85% payment rate for qualifying PTA/OTA-furnished services and modifiers.

sources.title: WebPT Releases 2025 State of Rehab Therapy Report
sources.what_it_supports: Rehab-therapy survey context on documentation burden and increasing interest in technology/AI to reduce operational friction.

sources.title: APTA Report Points to Hiring Challenges for Outpatient Practices Amid Growth
sources.what_it_supports: Outpatient PT-practice vacancy rates, job openings and labor scarcity context.

sources.title: Provident Perspectives: Investment & Consolidation in the Physical Therapy Sector
sources.what_it_supports: Historical PT platform transaction multiple range and the consolidation/fragmentation narrative; target EBITDA sizes are materially above most intended targets.

sources.title: Hinge Health claims analysis on fully insured populations
sources.what_it_supports: Company-sponsored, consultant-validated claims analysis reporting reduced utilization including physical or occupational therapy; evidence of a bounded digital-MSK substitution channel.

sources.title: A Physical Therapy Profile: Demographics of the Profession, 2025
sources.what_it_supports: Professional workforce demographic and local-shortage context; it does not report a private-practice owner age distribution.

ai_replaceable_share.rationale: Documentation, administrative and revenue-cycle work are AI-exposed, whereas licensed, hands-on clinical time remains the paid product. Reclaimed documentation time is a throughput opportunity rather than a clinical headcount assumption.
caveats: Role mix is supplied at five-digit 62134 rather than six-digit 621340.
Clinical-task fractions exclude a claim that AI replaces billable hands-on treatment.
Audiology and other residual subsegments may have different exposure.

role_judgments.rationale: AI can support documentation and plan preparation but not hands-on PT treatment or licensed judgment.
PT assistants perform licensed/certified care; only limited documentation and scheduling tasks are exposed.
Speech-language clinical work remains human-delivered; documentation is the exposed component.
Occupational-therapy hands-on assessment and treatment remain core; administrative preparation is exposed.
Aide room-turnover, check-in and scheduling support can be partially absorbed through workflow automation.
Medical administration, eligibility, prior authorization and routine claims workflow are highly exposed subject to review.
OTA clinical delivery is not assumed replaceable; limited documentation tasks are exposed.
Compliance reporting and operational analysis automate, while payer, staffing and referral management persist.
Reception, reminders, intake and scheduling are highly exposed.
Routine office and billing work is highly exposed with human review retained.
The residual mixes audiology, clinical-adjacent, marketing and administrative roles; exposure is heterogeneous.

pi_dist.rationale: A clinic can retain some documentation-to-throughput benefit, but fee schedules, authorisation and contracted payer rates give payers meaningful ability to capture efficiency.
caveats: CMS Medicare mechanics are a direct payer channel but do not describe every commercial or Medicaid contract.

pi_moat.rationale: Licensed staffing, referral relationships, local reputation and payer contracting provide more protection than generic administration, but vacancy pressure and referral concentration limit portability.
caveats: Sector consolidation evidence does not prove moat for a clinic whose referrals are physician-personal or payer-concentrated.

t50_years.rationale: Documentation and administrative AI are useful and software-delivered, but governed workflow integration, compliance review and payer processes slow effective adoption.
caveats: Vendor survey language indicates direction, not a representative effective-adoption series.

current_adoption_pct.rationale: AI interest is increasing, but current effective use across documentation and administration is conservatively below half of practices.
caveats: No reopened source provides a code-specific percentage of clinics using AI effectively.

historical_analogs.value: EHR, practice-management and speech-recognition adoption: documentation and revenue-cycle friction fall first, while licensed clinical care remains the billable service.
historical_analogs.rationale: The meaningful analogue is workflow digitization, not elimination of hands-on therapist visits.
historical_analogs.plausible_range: Staged workflow adoption over several years
caveats: The source is a vendor survey release and not a causal clinical-productivity study.

selection.rationale: No reopened source reports a six-digit private-practice owner age distribution, so a conservative generic estimate is used without claiming direct support.
caveats: Workforce age and owner age are different populations.

succession_shortage_documented.rationale: The reopened APTA material documents staffing shortages and workforce demographics, but not a code-specific owner succession shortage; the boolean is therefore conservatively false.
succession_shortage_documented.plausible_range: false unless direct owner-succession evidence is obtained
caveats: Do not convert therapist vacancy evidence into owner-succession evidence.

active_consolidators.rationale: Provident describes active strategic and financial PT consolidation and lists multiple national platform presences; six is a conservative count of relevant scaled buyer ecosystems.
caveats: Platform count does not establish active acquisition appetite for every local specialty or payer mix.

buy_mult.rationale: A 4.5x EBITDA entry estimate is deliberately far below the reopened historical $12M-plus PT platform transaction range, reflecting a normalized local target with management and payer risk.
caveats: S4 comparables are materially larger and mostly historical, so this is a conservative bounded estimate rather than an observed band-size transaction multiple. Verify local transaction data and normalize owner-therapist pay.

exit_mult.rationale: A 10.0x EBITDA exit is a conservative below-median extrapolation from the cited 10.4x-16.1x historical platform range and assumes real multi-site scale, diversified payer mix and retained clinicians.
caveats: The cited transactions had $12M-$175M EBITDA and cannot support a precise multiple for a smaller clinic group.

pricing_structure.value: Payer fee schedules and contracted per-visit or timed-service reimbursement, supplemented by commercial, workers' compensation, Medicaid, school-contract or cash-pay arrangements depending on subsegment.
pricing_structure.rationale: Payer and billing mechanics govern whether operational efficiency converts into retained contribution margin.
caveats: CMS is not a full description of commercial, Medicaid, school or audiology economics.

cross_checks.uplift_vs_A_consistent: Yes. A moderate, back-office-led labor surface and four-year adoption estimate imply incremental throughput and administration gains, not sudden removal of licensed clinical labor.

terminal_value.justification: Hands-on licensed rehabilitation remains a paid service, though digital MSK programs create a bounded low-acuity substitution and payer-capture risk.

cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed: No. The workforce mix is five-digit, CMS applies to Medicare rather than all reimbursement, platform comparable transactions are far larger, and the small-practice multiple is an asking SDE metric; all are disclosed on relevant inputs.
cross_checks.pricing_model_deflation: Payer rate-setting, authorization and visit controls can capture documentation-driven efficiency. That mechanism is scored in capture only and is not used to make clinical work AI-replaceable.

dataset_mismatch.description: The supplied workforce mix is five-digit 62134 while the record is six-digit 621340; deterministic labor-share vintages also differ across wages and receipts.
sensitivity: Audiology/hearing-aid mix can have different margin and labor economics from therapy visits.
The n_band includes 100-499 and 500+ employer classes, which is broader than a typical local clinic group.

market_boundary.selection_basis: The frozen prompt's payer, workforce, consolidation and digital-MSK evidence is most directly relevant to outpatient PT; other named code subsegments are disclosed as material heterogeneity.

top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: Changes the adoption factor because effective workflow deployment is not measured directly.
Determines the modest value surface; counting licensed clinical time as headcount removal would overstate it.
Creates or removes the consolidation spread, but evidence spans small listings and much larger historical platforms.

reviewer_note: Reopen the CMS, WebPT, Provident and Hinge sources. Check that clinical labor is not treated as replaceable, payer capture is not double-counted with terminal demand, and the buy/exit multiple bridge is kept explicitly caveated.
